data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - The Gavel Falls On Global Tariffs As Inflationary Fears Return To The Fold
data.description:
Week in review - Tariffs and inflation

  • The US Supreme Court struck down the administration's broad global tariffs under an emergency act (IEEPA). However, the administration is pivoting to alternative legal tools.
  • US financial markets reacted positively to the tariff ruling but had to contend with a disappointing 1.4% GDP report and higher-than-expected inflation data (PCE price index up 0.4% MoM).
  • A 5% weekly gain in oil prices, combined with persistent inflation data, has brought inflationary pressures back to the fore.
  • The market's attention shifts to high-stakes corporate earnings, especially Nvidia, as the bellwether for the AI boom and crucial economic data, including the Australian Monthly CPI and US Consumer Confidence.

When looking back at the week that was, there is no place better to start than the Supreme Court ruling in the United States.

In a significant legal blow to the administration, the US Supreme Court ruled on February 20, 2026, that President Trump exceeded his constitutional authority by using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to bypass Congress and impose broad global tariffs.

While the 6–3 decision effectively strikes down the legal justification for many of the administration's "Liberation Day" and fentanyl-related levies, it does not mean the end of trade restrictions.

The ruling specifically targets the use of emergency statutes for taxation, yet leaves intact several other tariffs, such as those on steel, aluminum, and certain auto parts that are grounded in different legal frameworks like Section 232 or Section 301.

Consequently, while the court has dismantled the specific "emergency" scaffolding the President relied upon, the administration has already signaled it will pivot to alternative statutory tools to keep its wider trade agenda standing.

What is the way forward?

The Supreme Court’s decision focused strictly on the boundaries of executive power rather than the merits of trade protectionism, meaning the administration's broader tariff objectives remain very much alive. While the previous legal justifications have been stripped away, they are being rapidly replaced by new statutory foundations, ushering in a volatile transition period for the global economy.

Businesses now face a period of deep instability as they navigate the unlikely prospect of receiving full refunds for past duties and the high probability of "replacement tariffs" that will restore costs to their previous levels.

Ultimately, while the specific legal "scaffolding" has been dismantled, the administration is already rebuilding its trade barriers; regardless of the court's stance on the Constitution, the era of high tariffs appears far from over.

How did markets react?

US financial markets reacted positively to the Supreme Court's decision on Friday, with all three major indexes climbing immediately following the ruling and securing a winning week overall.

While investors were encouraged by the legal blow to the administration’s trade barriers, they also had to weigh a disappointing GDP report showing growth slowing to 1.4% alongside higher-than-expected inflation data.

Treasury yields moved upward as the loss of tariff revenue sparked concerns over a widening fiscal deficit and increased bond supply.

Internationally, the optimism was even more pronounced; Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) index surged to a new all-time high, and gold prices continued their ascent as a weakening dollar and persistent geopolitical uncertainty fueled demand for safe-haven assets.

The continued rise in Gold prices is a nod to the uncertainty which Friday's decision brings despite the optimism. In short, the decision in some ways brings more questions than answers.

Inflation back to the fore

The US PCE price index rose 0.4% month-over-month in December 2025, following a 0.2% increase in November, the most since February and above market expectations of 0.3%.

This, coupled with a 5% weekly gain for Oil prices, has reignited fears of inflationary pressures returning, which would complicate matters for the global economy.

In December 2025, the European Central Bank projected that a 14% spike in oil prices would potentially add 0.5 percentage points to eurozone inflation over the long term while shaving a modest 0.1 percentage points off annual growth.

This outlook is particularly sensitive to Europe’s heavy reliance on imported energy, which often causes the Euro to weaken against the Dollar as fuel costs rise. Since those projections were made, oil prices have already climbed by that exact 14% margin, bringing the ECB’s cautionary scenario into reality.

Meanwhile, across the Atlantic, the Federal Reserve expressed concern in its January 2026 meeting minutes about the persistent risk of inflation remaining above target, even suggesting that future interest rate hikes could be necessary.

Despite these hawkish signals from central bankers, investors appear relatively unfazed, continuing to price in two rate cuts for the year, a sentiment supported by the fact that domestic gasoline prices remain near multi-year lows, providing a crucial psychological buffer for the markets.

If oil prices do continue to rise and the geopolitical situation in the Middle East continues to escalate, there may be real risks to the inflationary outlook moving forward.

The Week Ahead

The week ahead sees the focus shift from macroeconomic policy debates to high-stakes corporate earnings and inflation data.

The AI Litmus Test: Nvidia and Software Earnings

The primary catalyst for Wall Street will be Nvidia’s (NVDA) quarterly report. As the bellwether for the artificial intelligence boom, Nvidia’s results and guidance will determine if the massive valuations in the semiconductor sector remain justified.

Beyond hardware, the spotlight moves to the "software layer" of AI. Results from Salesforce (CRM), Snowflake (SNOW), Intuit (INTU), Zoom (ZM), and Zscaler (ZS) will be scrutinized to see if enterprise spending on AI software is finally translating into significant revenue growth. These reports follow a period of market stagnation caused by hawkish Fed signals and rising geopolitical tensions.

Central Banks and the "balancing act" Dilemma

Central banks face a difficult balancing act, often described as a choice between fighting persistent inflation and supporting slowing growth.

United States: On Wednesday, the CB Consumer Confidence Index will be a vital indicator of whether high interest rates and inflation are finally breaking the American consumer’s resilience.

Australia: Wednesday also brings the Monthly CPI indicator. If inflation remains above the RBA’s target band, markets may price in a higher probability of a March rate hike.

Japan: The market will watch a speech by BoJ’s Takada on Thursday for hints regarding the timing of further policy normalization, alongside Friday's Industrial Production and Retail Sales data.

Energy and Geopolitics

Rising oil prices (with Brent Crude seeing recent volatility) remain a wildcard. Tensions between the US and Iran have added a risk premium to energy markets, complicating the inflation outlook for central banks. Investors will monitor whether energy costs continue to climb, potentially forcing a "higher for longer" interest rate environment.

Summary of Key Dates:

Feb 24 (Tue): US Factory Orders.

Feb 25 (Wed): Nvidia Earnings; AU Monthly CPI; US Consumer Confidence.

Feb 26 (Thu): Salesforce & Snowflake Earnings; BoJ Takada Speech.

Feb 27 (Fri): Japan Industrial Production; US Jobless Claims.

Market Sentiment: Expect a "show me the money" attitude from investors. If AI leaders fail to beat high expectations or if inflation prints hot in Australia and Europe, the current market divergence, where the ASX 200 (AS51) has outperformed lagging US indices, could widen.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

From a technical perspective, the US dollar index (DXY) has had an impressive week and rally, which finally ran out of steam around the 98.00 handle.

Without a daily candle close above the 98.00 handle, the overall bearish trend remains intact despite the strong rally.

If the DXY is able to break higher, there is a confluence level just above which houses the 100- and 200-day MA and rests around the 98.50 handle.

It will be an intriguing week for the US dollar as markets fully digest the Supreme Court tariff decision and the Trump administration's response.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart, February 20, 2026

data.title: Coupang: Data Breach Costs Two Years
data.description:
Introduction

Coupang (CPNG) was hit by a massive data breach affecting over 33 million customers, which the Korean regulator attributed to poor cybersecurity. The impact on the shares was quick, with a 50% drop from the recent high. The question on my mind is, do I sell or buy more? Thus, I updated the valuation matrix and decided to buy more. The data breach may cost CPNG substantial fines and operating costs, but it has not led to the extinction of its brand. The consensus has incorporated a drop in margin growth that delays absolute earnings levels by about two years.

Breach Impact

As amply reported in the media, the company suffered a massive data breach with more than 33 million customer accounts exposed. The regulators have found Coupang at fault and will probably impose fines and remedies that will hurt margins. As discussed in previous articles, CPNG was steadily increasing fees and gaining scale, which would lead to substantial improvements in monetizing its e-commerce platform in Korea. This will likely be delayed or reduced in magnitude.

The new consensus figures seem to incorporate this margin hit, with cash earnings margins (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) growing 100 bp vs 200 bp previously, or from 5.4% to 4.6% in 2026. This has a nearly $400m cut to previously estimated cash earnings, which cuts growth from 70% in YE26 to 39%. By most metrics, this is still quite good growth and margin expansion. The risk to revenue seems muted with a modest estimated decline, and the most recent data see CPNG neck and neck with Naver (NHNCF).

4Q25 Results Preview

Since the data breach occurred in January 2026, the 4Q25 results should not be affected by reputational issues, customer discounting, etc. The market expects continued trends from the year, with Korean operations growing at around 10% in revenue and steady margin gains. The developing markets, including Taiwan, are a growth focus, and CPNG has sacrificed margins to gain market share. Post-breach, I would not expect the growth strategy to change, but the Korean market may suffer from attrition and higher costs, as outlined above in the new consensus estimates for full 2026.

Financial Estimates

The full impact of the breach is being incorporated into lower margins by the consensus, as discussed earlier. It’s still guesswork, as we don’t know how much the added cybersecurity will cost Coupang, while delayed fee increases and scale gains may offset much of the cost/margin loss from 2026 forward. The 2025 estimates also seem to factor in some 4Q25 weakness attributable to operations outside Korea. The new consensus continues to expect gradual margin gains and increasing earnings and FCF. The company is net cash, so any fines or restitution should not damage the balance sheet. Long term, by 2028, net income is still forecast to increase by more than 4x on under 7% EBITDA margins.

Valuation

Given the lower absolute earnings estimates for 2026-2028 and the growth rate, I reduce the YE26 price target to $35 from $52, maintaining the 1x PEG target. This is in line with the consensus $31 and 0.9x PEG or 31x P/CE (cash earnings). The company will need to deliver on these reduced estimates, rectify cybersecurity issues, and pay fines. I expect continued volatility through 2H26 as results and news flow normalize.

Peer Comps

As with most equity analysis, I compare the company to a broad peer group to measure growth rates and valuation metrics. The e-commerce sector seems discounted relative to medium-term cash earnings growth rates; most trade at under 0.7x PEG or 12x P/CE, which may be due to crowding out or rotation into the AI trade.

Risk

Coupang operates in a highly competitive market for a digitally astute customer base that has made monetization or margin gains difficult. This continued environment could reduce the expected margin and subsequent earnings growth. The data breach may cause reputational damage and force CPNG to discount customers or reduce traffic/revenue, which could cut into margins and the earnings outlook.

Conclusion

I rate CPNG a Buy. The company suffered a significant reputational hit that will most likely result in reduced margins and slower growth in 2026 and 2027. However, the share price decline appears to over-incorporate this new lower-earnings scenario, with a 100% upside and a risk-return balance worth the risk.

data.title: First Solar: Buy The Dip, Heavy Growth, Strong Demand, Strategic Moves
data.description:
First Solar Inc. (FSLR) had seen steady gains for nine months since the tariff announcements that shocked markets last April. Over the last 8 weeks or so, the stock has scaled back. Earnings are arriving in a few days to wrap up 2025 results, and this renewable semiconductor stock seems to be in an interesting position as demand for their product continues to rise. FSLR is a leading U.S.-based solar technology and energy solutions company primarily focused on the design, manufacture, and sale of advanced photovoltaic (PV) solar modules and large-scale solar power systems. They specialize in thin-film cadmium telluride (CdTe) solar panels, which offer a lower-carbon profile and competitive cost structure for utility-scale solar installations across domestic and international markets. They primarily sell to utilities, independent power producers, and commercial and industrial companies.

FSLR is recognized as the largest thin‑film solar panel producer in the world, with almost 50% of global market share. The stock is up about 48% over one year but has scaled back roughly 13% since the beginning of 2026. This pullback has pushed me to a buy rating for FSLR with Q4 earnings on the horizon. Outside of a slightly damper balance sheet adjustment, earnings continue to fly, profits and cash are strong, valuation is steady, and they are the global leader in a healthy, growing market.

Soaring Growth

Growth has been exceptional for FSLR. EPS has soared by a compounded annual growth rate of 44.2% over 5 years. They nearly doubled their sales YoY, reaching a record $1.594 billion last quarter. Analysts expect earnings next week to arrive at $5.22, almost a dollar higher than their already record-breaking prior quarter. In Q4 2022, they were unprofitable, posting $-0.07 EPS. This growth is phenomenal, and I suspect them to beat earnings again, as growth has been fueled by heightened demand for solar modules worldwide, and the firm remains strong in backlog. Last quarter they reported contracted backlog for future module sales of 53.7 GW, valued at 16.4 billion through 2030. The growth does not stop here for FSLR.

Healthy Margins

FSLR wasn’t consistently profitable until 2023, as capital expenditures continued to ramp up as management could see a clear path toward strong industry demand. Free cash flow margin has finally broken out as cash from operations exploded last quarter to $1.27 billion, from being negative in the same quarter last year. This has been a result of a flipped script on accounts receivable to the green, more module sales, and improved gross margins on each sale.

Gross margin last quarter was down YoY as management noted higher costs in some areas and lower tax credits; however, has still seen a great trend of margin results. Net income margin at 27.7% towers over the sector median of 5.4%. They continue to optimize their industry-leading cadmium‑telluride (CdTe) thin‑film modules through vertical integration by expanding manufacturing footprint and process innovation. I don’t even necessarily need to see more growth on margins; I just want to see them remain steady, with their already high market share and growing module sales.

Valuation Stable

After a stretch of unprofitability and inconsistency, FSLR now shows a maturing and more normalized valuation profile. Trailing PE is 18x, and forward is 16x, both lower than the sector median, and as you can see by the chart above, extremely stable. Through the noted earnings reports and some up-and-down price movement, you can see how the stocks PE has remained relatively flat, suggesting a fair valuation. I think, based on the price/book also being below the industry, that FSLR is slightly undervalued. Also, assuming they come in with a strong EPS as analysts predict, I think in a week from now, FSLR will be appealing at an even further discount.

Track the Balance Sheet

First Solar’s balance sheet remains financially strong and conservatively positioned, but some key metrics show moderate changes over time. The company continues to hold a substantial cash and short‑term investment base of over $2 billion, which exceeds its reported total debt of around $890 million, leaving it in a net cash position, supporting liquidity, operational flexibility, and room for further capital expenditure.

Debt to equity has crept higher over the last few years to support capacity expansion and manufacturing buildouts but is still quite low among peers and is still under 10%. Similarly, their quick ratio has diminished during this expansionary period, now down to 1.38, but it still reflects liquid assets meaningfully exceeding short‑term liabilities.

I would consider the dampening balance sheet a small risk to this stock, but even that is hard to justify. Cash position and assets are still in a solid position, and I won’t be concerned unless this starts to flip negative or debt to equity rises more toward 30% in upcoming quarters.

Expansion & Demand

FSLR continues to strategically expand its manufacturing footprint to meet rapidly growing global solar demand. The company is adding a fifth U.S. factory with approximately 3.7 GW of annual module capacity, expected to begin production in late 2026. They have had recent growth already in Alabama and Louisiana and are capturing policy incentives and tariff avoidance from greater domestic manufacturing. They currently do not manufacture in China, which has become a strategic advantage amongst competitors paying high tariffs, which has led to their nice profitability. The unique sustainability profile and performance characteristics of thin‑film technology that FSLR has pairs nicely with the demand that is being driven by corporate and government renewable energy targets and grid decarbonization goals.

Conclusion

FSLR has corrected just in time for what I suspect will be solid Q4 earnings. The stock is already valued fair to discounted, which could look cheaper with a strong report. Their balance sheet has been a little rocky, but they are still in a great cash position and have strong margins to fall back on. Higher module sales will continue on higher demand, and their already efficient production looks like it could continue to strengthen through vertical integration and expansion in domestic capacity. Buy this dip from FSLR.

data.title: Silvercorp Metals: What Silver Price Is Embedded In Today's $2.43B Valuation?
data.description:
December Baseline

In December, I valued Silvercorp Metals Inc. (SVM) at approximately $1.374 billion using a conservative sum-of-the-parts approach. At that time, SVM traded at $7.83, market cap was ~$1.59 billion, and my take was a neutral Hold.

My thesis was simple: China was producing solid cash flow, El Domo was still under construction, so I discounted it by 45%, and its market valuation left no margin of safety.

Today, however, SVM share price is $10.98, up 40%; market cap is $2.43 billion, Enterprise value (EV) is $2.24 billion - and management clearly says that the upside came from aggressively higher silver price then, which again experienced an extreme spike to $118.70 in January before retracing toward ~$77.

The key question is not where silver traded at its January peak, but what sustained silver price regime would justify a $2.43 billion valuation on a forward basis.

Q3 Operating Inputs

From their Q3 2026 results announced last week, SVM’s revenue was $126.11 million, up 50.8% year over year. After-smelter realized silver prices were $49.0/oz, whereas AISC (net of by-products) was $12.86/oz:

All-in sustaining cost per ounce of silver ("AISC") (net of by-product credits): $12.86, remaining flat with $12.75 in Q3 Fiscal 2025;

72% of the quarter’s revenue came from silver, while other associated metals like gold etc produced the rest. However, management has clearly stated that performance was mainly driven by an 80% hike in silver prices [this was in Q3: silver spot prices reached an unprecedented ~$118.70 in January, to fall back ~30% to $77 today. I have carefully used the Q3 framework all through].

Also note that their quarterly silver production was ~1.9 million oz, which is ~7.6 million oz annualized.

Isolate China Operating Value

SVM’s current EV is ~$2.24 billion, which already reflects the company’s net cash position because EV = Market Cap – Net Cash (if net cash positive). Thus, the $2.24 billion figure represents the market value of operating assets after accounting for balance sheet cash.

Using that value, let us try to figure out how much China represented in operating value. For that, subtract non-China components, so the ~$245 million equity portfolio, El Domo, assuming invested capital only, whose construction budget has now increased to $284 million, and marginal amounts from the Kuanping expansion. Let us conservatively assign a combined value of ~$500 million. Therefore, China represents an EV of $2.24B − 0.50B ≈ $1.74B.

This is the value the market assigns to the China silver business of SVM.

Implied Sustainable China Cash Flow

Now, we will assume a mid-cycle multiple of 5.5x operating cash flow. That gives us an implied sustainable China OCF of:-

1.74B ÷ 5.5 ≈ $316M annually (using silver-dominant economics for simplification)

Let us take a range of $300–320 million, which is the cash flow embedded in the current valuation.

Convert Implied OCF to Silver Price

In order to convert this implied OCF into a measurable silver price value, let us take:

  • Annual silver production = 7.6M oz

  • AISC = $12.86/oz

  • X = sustained realized silver price

Therefore, silver margin per oz = X − 12.86

Annual silver margin:

7.6M × (X − 12.86)

If we equate this to $300 million, the lower value of our earlier OCF range, we get:

7.6M × (X − 12.86) = 300M

Solving for X, we have:

X − 12.86 = 39.5X ≈ $52.36 per ounce

That means the current valuation implies sustained realized silver in the low $50s.

Observe that even with spot silver currently around ~$77 following its $118.70 January spike, SVM’s present valuation still back-solves to a low-$50s sustained regime. That suggests the equity market itself is treating recent spot levels as transitory rather than structural. If the market believed $77 silver was durable, SVM would mathematically imply a materially higher embedded silver price than ~$52–53.

Sensitivity Analysis

Given the inputs we previously had, assumed constant here, we will apply three dynamic multiples to account for bull/bear scenarios. So, we will take 4.5x for a down-cycle compression scenario, 5.5x as the current cycle implied multiple, and 6.0x for a bull scenario.

This gives us the following sensitivity table:

Now, let us convert this back to equity value. So, we will add back non-China assets (~$500M) to China EV. This will give us various values. To take one example, at $53 silver, 5.5x multiple, we have $1.70B as China EV, and adding back the $0.50B non-China assets, we get $2.20B as implied EV. This is close to the current EV of $2.24B, which is why we derived embedded silver at around $52-53.

Now, how do we interpret this table? For the downsize case, look at the second row, with silver at $45, and a compressed 4.5x multiple. Here, China EV is $1.05B, adding non-China assets gives us an implied EV of $1.55. Versus current EV of $2.24B, this implies a ~30% EV downside.

Take the mid-case, with silver at $50 and 5.5x multiple. Here, China EV is $1.55B, so implied EV is $2.05B, slightly below current EV.

The embedded case, with silver at $53 and a 5.5x multiple, gives us fair value. This shows mild optimism, given the actual silver value was $49 at the time.

Now take a normalized high case where silver stabilizes around $60 — materially below the January spike of $118.70 but above prior mid-cycle norms - and apply a bullish 6x multiple. That gives us a China EV of $2.2B and an implied EV of $2.7B. This represents a 15-20% upside from current SVM levels.

The simple mathematical conclusion we can draw here is that downside convexity is higher than upside convexity. In other words, despite spot silver near ~$77, the market valuation still implies a sustained low-$50s regime. That leaves very little upside if silver rises further, but a much larger downside if it goes down. Therefore, if you are betting on SVM at these prices, you are effectively betting on silver sustaining elevated levels after one of the most volatile rallies in the past decade.

Conclusion: Hold

As the math here shows, meaningful upside requires silver to stabilize durably at elevated levels rather than reverting toward the $45–55 range implied by the current valuation framework.

Now, the 2025 silver rally came from a combination of gold momentum spillover. There was also higher industrial demand, mainly solar. There has been limited growth in primary mines in recent years, which constrained supply and helped drive up prices. Finally, there was a high volume of speculation in the market.

However, silver exhibits higher mean reversion than gold, so it often goes down once marginal demand falls. Its industrial usage makes it more price-sensitive than gold. Mines also tend to produce more as demand increases, taking prices down eventually. Thus, while silver can spike well above historical norms on momentum - as evidenced by the recent move above $100 - the probability of it stabilizing at such extremes remains lower than a consolidation in the $45-55 range.

That being the case, our valuation should be anchored in a more conservative base case. Unless the investor has a strong conviction that silver can remain structurally elevated well above prior mid-cycle norms, SVM offers limited upside relative to downside risk under mean reversion. Thus, the appropriate stance at $10.98 is Hold.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: IPO Market Stays Fairly Quiet During The Short Holiday Week
data.description:
One small issuer and four blank check companies went public this past week as the IPO market continued to weather the recent volatility.

Facial recognition software provider Rank One Computing (ROC) upsized and priced at the high end to raise $24 million at a $114 million market cap. ROC operates in an intensely competitive space, developing proprietary AI software for biometric identification, video analytics, and digital evidence analysis, in addition to providing R&D services. The company serves both government agencies and regulated commercial sectors. ROC finished up 3%.

The week’s four blank check IPOs were led by Averin Capital Acquisition (ACAAU), which raised $250 million. Led by former Flagship partner David Berry, the SPAC plans to target businesses at the intersection of technology and health.

5 IPOs During the Week of February 16th, 2026
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 02/20

ROC (ROC)

 $24M $114M 9% +3% +3%
Provides AI-powered facial recognition and biometric verification software.

Averin Capital Acq. (ACAAU)

 $250M $315M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company backed by Averin Capital targeting health and technology businesses.

Paloma Acquisition I (PALOU)

 $150M $193M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company targeting gold, silver, and other precious metals.

Armada Acquisition III (AACIU)

 $225M $308M 0% +0% +0%
Third blank check company led by the former CEO of Cantaloupe Inc. targeting fintech, SaaS, and AI.

Abony Acquisition I (AACOU)

 $200M $273M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company targeting defense, advanced computing, software, and media.

A couple smaller names joined the pipeline, led by medtech Medical 21 (MAVG), which filed to raise $25 million. Four SPACs also submitted initial filings, led by two that plan to raise $175 million: NewHold Enterprises’ NewHold Investment IV (NHIVU) and energy-focused Dynamix IV (DYNXU).

6 Filings During the Week of February 16th, 2026
Issuer Business Deal Size Sector Lead Underwriter

Forefront Tech Acq. (FTHAU)

 $100M SPAC BTIG
Blank check company targeting blockchain-enabled AI, digital trade identities, and robotics.

Medical 21 (MAVG)

 $25M Health Care ThinkEquity
Developing a synthetic regenerative graft for coronary bypass surgery.

NewHold Investment IV (NHIVU)

 $175M SPAC BTIG
Blank check company formed by NewHold Enterprises targeting industrial technology.

Dynamix IV (DYNXU)

 $175M SPAC Cohen & Company
Blank check company targeting the energy and power value chain.

EIR Biopharma (EIRB.RC)

 $17M Health Care Kingswood
Preclinical biotech developing in-licensed therapies for eye diseases.

Plutonian Acquisition II (PLUNU)

 $100M SPAC A.G.P.
Blank check company targeting energy storage, telecom, and consumer sectors.

Week Ahead

No deals are currently on the calendar for the week ahead, although some small deals may join the calendar throughout the week, as the typical February lull takes hold of the IPO market while issuers work on year-end audited financials.

While recent IPO jitters may scare some potential issuers, a number of sizable names in the active pipeline are eligible to launch in the week ahead, including fleet management software provider Motive (MTVE), which could raise an estimated $600 million. Other notable names that could set terms include Medtronic’s diabetes carve-out MiniMed Group (MMED), asthma-focused AI biotech Generate Biomedicines (GENB), and crypto investment platform Grayscale (GRAY).

Street research is expected for four companies in the week ahead, and four lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 2/19/2026, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 2.0% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 is up 0.4%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include CoreWeave (CRWV) and Kenvue (KVUE). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 17.6% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 9.0%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kioxia and Galderma.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Last Time Non-U.S. Ruled The Roost
data.description:
The rotation out of US equities is in its second year and going strong. The rest of the world may be having its share of problems, but underperforming stocks is not one of them. Here is a brief snapshot of how things are going in other parts of the globe, relative to the flattish ways of the S&P 500 thus far.

This got us to thinking about the last time non-US equities gripped the imagination and enthusiasm of the investing crowd. Let’s take a little trip back to the quaint world of this century’s first decade.

Swan Song for the Global Age

In hindsight, we know that it was the last gasp of the Global Age – the final five years of the quarter century in which the neoliberal trinity of open borders, light-touch regulation and unconstrained capital reigned as the unapologetic Zeitgeist. But for those of us living in those years, sandwiched in between the dot-com crash and the global financial crisis, 2003 to 2007 was just another chapter in the “end of history,” briefly interrupted from late-1990s good vibes by said bursting of the tech bubble, but with the Clinton-era directive to not stop thinking about tomorrow still circulating endlessly in our limbic regions.

The US dollar, which had risen steadily during the world’s love affair with everything dot-com, held its own during the 2001 recession but started to falter the following year, setting the stage for what would become the great non-US equity rally of the mid-aughts.

What caused the decline of the dollar and the related move out of dollar-denominated assets? Several variables were probably at play, including the simple fact that the late-90s tech boom was almost entirely a US affair, so a “sell America” impulse was natural when that play fell out of favor. But there was more to it than that. The outperformance by international equities of almost every stripe over their US counterparts was solid, structural and sustained over this entire five-year period.

This was the period when China’s fast-growing economy blasted into its supercycle phase. Observers suddenly realized that all those jobs which had left the US due to offshoring in the 1980s and 1990s were not reappearing all over the world as hubs in an increasingly complex global supply chain. The word of the day was “decoupling,” as in the growing independence of these emerging regional clusters without the need for depending on capricious portfolio investment from Western capitals. The non-US world, and especially the emerging parts of that world, was destined to grow faster than the established West. Faster growth would mean more prosperity and, sooner or later, companies that would be every bit as good as, if not better than, their Western counterparts.

Decoupling Arrives, Twenty Years Later

Things didn’t work out that way, as we now know. The global financial crisis knocked all stock markets for a nasty loop, and the Great Recession set in. The Eurozone nearly fell apart in the early 2010s. China’s supercycle ran out of steam in the middle of that decade, prompting a major devaluation of its currency and a collapse in Chinese equities. Meanwhile, central banks, led by the US Fed, took over the job of rebuilding the economy by providing ultra-cheap money. That easy money, in turn, catalyzed animal spirits in Silicon Valley to invest in audacious tech projects, some of which became extremely successful. US assets were back in favor, led by the tech giants and some of the smaller pilot fish that swam along in their wake.

Finally, though, the decoupling moment that had been imagined back in the mid-2000s arrived last year. The old Washington Consensus of a global village unified under the neoliberal banner of free trade, judiciously overseen by the US, is gone. China is ascendant, Europe is regrouping into a post-Pax Americana order, and even Japan is showing some moxie again as the country rallies to its new and extremely popular prime minister. What all of this is going to look like five years from now is anybody’s guess. But the composition of global asset markets is likely, we think, to be quite a bit more diversified than it is today.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Continue To Decline
data.description:
Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends. Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values continued to trend higher in December, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values continued to decline, remaining at their lowest level in nearly five years. Last month's ZHVI came in at $357,445, up 0.21% from the previous month and up 0.16% from one year ago. After adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.01% month-over-month and -3.00% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates”, its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is at an all-time high.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.66% off its all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI are "the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index Home Price Index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction. Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are “substantially different from non-distressed sales” and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents “two very different market segments”. Eric Stubbs, Ph.D., at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales is a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fourth Quarter Real GDP: Reduced Due To Government Shutdown
data.description:
The real GDP of the U.S. was impacted in the fourth quarter of 2025 by the shutdown of the government. The annualized growth rate for the quarter was 1.4 percent.

Here is the picture....

The Bureau of Economic Analysis described this disturbance in its first estimate of the 4th quarter and year 2025.

The decline in the government contribution: "Due to a lapse in appropriations, some federal government agencies were closed, and some employees were furloughed from October 1 through November 12. The full effects of the partial federal government shutdown on the fourth-quarter estimates cannot be quantified because they are embedded in the regular source data that underlie the estimates and cannot be separately identified."

As far as the whole year is concerned, the U.S. economy grew by 2.2 percent.

This is a little less than what the economy had been achieving in recent quarters, but it is not far off the picture for the past sixteen years.

The growth of the U.S., I have been arguing, has been growing, primarily driven by the supply side of the economy. The Federal Reserve has been operating with its focus on the "quantitative" aspect of monetary policy by working basically on its portfolio of securities.

This has allowed the economy to proceed along a path that is consistent with the rise in the productive capacity of the country.

This is why you see the picture above so flat.

The "swing" in the 2020-2022 period was generated by the COVID-19 dilemma and subsequent recession.

But, as can be seen, the growth rate proceeding from this disruption returned to roughly the same growth path as was taking place before that time.

And predictions for the future have centered around a 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent growth rate in 2026 and beyond.

Note one other thing about this picture.

The economic growth of the U.S. from the end of the Great Recession in 2009 up until the recession of 2020 represents the longest period of economic expansion in U.S. history.

Note too that the recession in 2020...just two months... March and April 2020...is the shortest recession in U.S. history.

The latest figures from the BEA support the belief that the economy is still being driven by the supply side of the economy and will continue to be driven by this side of the economy in the near future.

This is a strong reason for the belief that the government spending in the U.S. will return to previous levels (or higher) and the economic growth of the United States will continue to be in the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range in the near future.

data.title: AMD's Pullback Won't Last Long (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Something fundamental has shifted in the AMD (AMD) story over the last quarter and I don't think the market is grasping it yet. The conversation is no longer about incremental GPU improvements or beating the competition in a benchmark. It's about control, control of the rack-scale architecture, control of the supply chain and control of full-stack AI.

They made it very clear that 2026 will be about integrated rack solutions, not accelerators. That's a fundamental shift. They are positioning themselves to go up the value chain from a silicon supplier to an infrastructure supplier. If they succeed, the multiple expands. If they don't the multiple resets.

Rack-Scale: Where Strategy Meets Execution

The management focused on the fact that the majority of MI450 revenue in 2026 will come from rack-scale solutions, not from 8-GPU server solutions. Now that's not a product segmentation comment. That's a strategic comment.

Shipping a GPU is one thing. It's about the performance of the silicon and the yield of the silicon and the supply of the silicon. Shipping a rack is a completely different story. It's about the orchestration of the solution. It's about synchronizing the compute trays and the HBM memory and the networking and the firmware and the software stacks. Revenue is not recognized until the rack ships.

It's a more complex operating model. But I think it's a higher value-capture model. Rack-scale plays take AMD from a components player to an infrastructure player. If Helios (part of AI data center segment) executes well in the second half of 2026, AMD gets a different type of multiple. If execution issues arise, the market adjusts fast. This is not an evolutionary change but a step-change.

EPYC: The AI Multiplier Investors Overlook

While the GPU discussion receives all the attention, I think the CPU discussion is equally relevant. Lisa Su explicitly linked EPYC sales to agentive AI workloads, those environments where orchestration, preprocessing, and task parallelization are increasing along with GPU clusters.

AI clusters are not GPU-only environments. They need high-end CPUs to run the coordination and orchestration on the head nodes and execution on the compute nodes. If this plays out, CPU demand will increase with the growth in AI infrastructure rather than being cannibalized by the growth in AI infrastructure. Fifth-gen EPYC drove more than half of the total available market in the server space in Q4, and the company saw strengthening server order trends even through the softness in the season.

Venice is launching later this year, which extends the product cadence. If the server TAM grows at strong double-digit rates in 2026 and AMD continues to take share, then EPYC becomes an AI multiplier. The same hyperscaler spend drives both GPU and CPU sales. Having both stabilizes the business and reduces the concentration risks.

Software: Weakness to Good Enough

ROCm may not be at the same level as CUDA in terms of software stack maturity. I don't think this is up for debate. However, I don't think perfect is the standard by which we evaluate this situation. Management pointed out the ability to support leading models at day zero, upstream support with inference engines such as vLLM, and the introduction of an enterprise-grade AI suite to reduce friction in the software stack.

The economics of inference are moving towards the cost per token. Hyperscalers need optionality. They do not need parity across every benchmark, they need stability and predictability. Software development is an incremental process. It does not make headlines. Friction reduction compounds over time. If ROCm becomes operationally reliable across key workloads, AMD's addressable market expands considerably. That's an important but low-key driver.

Supply Planning and Operational Discipline

The supply of HBM and advanced packaging has been a constraint for the industry for two years. AMD has been very open about addressing the constraint head-on. The company has been planning for a significant ramp for multiple years now and does not expect supply to be an issue with the rollout of its AI initiative.

The company has been increasing its inventory levels due to Data Center demand. This is the kind of situation that should raise many caution flags in a normal semiconductor industry cycle. In the context of a structural AI infrastructure cycle, it could represent planning discipline. The difference lies only in the execution. If the racks roll out smoothly in both Q3 and Q4, it's a sign of prudent supply planning.

If the supply chain does not perform as well as the company does, the entire story will change instantly. This is not a normal semiconductor industry channel restocking cycle. This is a hyperscaler infrastructure build-out cycle. That makes a big difference.

Valuation: Growth-Backed, Not Hype-Driven

The current stock price of $202 translates into a forward non-GAAP EPS multiple of about 31x based on the consensus estimate of $6.7 per share of EPS for 2026. However, this figure shrinks significantly to a forward EPS multiple of 20x when considering the 2027 estimate of $10.6 per share of EPS. This essentially translates into the fact that, should the company be able to continue on this trajectory the valuation will normalize very quickly.

Consensus revenue is expected to reach approximately $46.4 billion in 2026 and increase to roughly $64.8 billion in 2027, representing approximately 38-39% year-over-year growth. Beyond that, revenue is projected to compound at roughly 30% annually through 2028. Over the same period, the forward price-to-sales ratio declines from around 7.3x based on 2026 estimates to approximately 5.2x on 2027 projections. On a PEG basis, the stock screens attractively given the expected EPS acceleration and embedded operating leverage.

As the Data Center mix continues to grow and Rack Scale Systems contribute more dollar content per deployment, the company can grow earnings at a rate greater than its revenue growth. If the company is able to execute well with the MI450 and Helios products and the EPYC processor maintains its current level of demand, the earnings power for 2027 justifies a higher multiple than the current one, not a lower one. Indeed, the current valuation does not look expensive at all and it looks like an opportunity to buy into the beginning of a multi-year earnings growth cycle supporting my strong buy.

Risks To Watch

The first and biggest risk here is integration complexity, as rack-scale systems bring numerous points of potential failure, particularly with networking, firmware, validation, and packaging. A hiccup at any of these levels will impact revenue growth and confidence. The second risk is competitive pricing pressure, as accelerator supply chains improve and add supply. Margin pressure will then threaten the current growth trajectory.

The third risk is CPU decoupling, as GPU demand accelerates, and server CPU demand slows. AMD will then lose their multiplier. Lastly, moderation of Hyperscaler CapEx will directly impact AI infrastructure vendors like AMD. This cycle will be dependent on sustained large-scale investment.

Bottom Line

AMD is undertaking an important transition, from high-performance chip supplier to rack-scale AI infrastructure architect. If Helios and MI450 ramp up well, EPYC demand remains strong, and operating leverage appears in the second half, then earnings growth accelerates significantly into 2027 and beyond.

data.title: DNOW's Plunge Represents A Big Opportunity
data.description:
Feb. 20 was an awful day for DNOW Inc. (DNOW) shareholders. Shares of the company plummeted over 20.8% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. In addition to falling short of analysts’ expectations when it came to revenue and earnings per share, the business provided what I would consider to be an insufficient amount of data to have a full understanding of the picture of the company as it stands today and things moving forward. Normally, this would not be all that big a deal. Plenty of companies don't provide much in the way of forward-looking guidance. But considering that the business recently completed a massive transaction, absorbing MRC Global in an all-stock deal, and given that financial data provided by the company in the earnings release is not enough to piece together what the company will look like for 2026, I would say that management certainly fell short of that.

Even though I am critical about this decision, one thing I will say is that the stock looks incredibly cheap. Even if we assume that management can achieve the results that they mentioned when they first announced the merger with MRC Global in the middle of last year, the stock is drastically cheaper than other players that have similarities to it. This certainly justifies, in my opinion, a bullish outlook. I feel that way, especially now that we know that synergies are being captured at a faster rate than what had been previously anticipated. So at the end of the day, I believe that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate like I had it last year makes sense. And if management does come out with more comprehensive data, it wouldn't be unthinkable to upgrade the stock to a "Strong Buy."

A Rough Quarter

The final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was definitely interesting. During the quarter, for instance, the company reported $959 million of revenue. That's an increase of 68% over the $571 million the business reported a year earlier. At first glance, this looks fantastic. But unfortunately, all of that sales growth came from the company's purchase of MRC Global. That transaction closed on Nov. 6, bringing into the picture significant additional revenue and, ideally, profitability as time goes on. Management did detail that, in the United States, it received $298 million worth of revenue because of MRC Global. And when it came to international markets, which would be those that exclude the United States and Canada, we can add another $90 million to the top line.

Even with this contribution, however, the company fell short of the expectations that analysts had. In fact, they were banking on revenue coming in $10.5 million above what was ultimately seen. This meant that revenue was flat year over year at $571 million. Even though the United States saw growth from $451 million to $467 million, international sales dipped from $54 million to $53 million. The bigger problem was Canada. Sales there plummeted from $66 million to $51 million.

Canada proved to be a pain point for the company all throughout 2025. Revenue contracted for that time to $214 million compared to the $253 million that was seen in 2024. It is important to note that, because of the company’s exposure to the oil and gas space, it is heavily influenced by the level of oil and gas drilling, completions, servicing, production, transmission, refining, and petrochemical activities that go on in these markets. Year over year for the third quarter, the number of active rigs in the United States dropped from 209 to 177.

Admittedly, the United States and other global markets also saw declines. But those drops were significantly lower at 7.8% for the United States and 6.2% for other international markets. This compares to the 15.3% drop that we saw in Canada. We don't have comprehensive details yet. But we do know that weakness during the first nine months of 2025 was also caused in part by unfavorable foreign currency fluctuations. Almost certainly, that continued into the final quarter as well.

The bottom line for the company was much more volatile. The business went from $0.21 in earnings per share to negative $0.95 in earnings per share year over year. This caused the company to fall short of expectations by $1.10 per share. As disastrous as this looks, it's not unexpected on my end. I say this because earnings per share often fluctuate drastically when companies are in the process of merging. There are almost always significant one-time costs. For instance, the company reported $0.33 in per-share charges related to the transaction directly during the quarter. It saw a $0.08 per share hit associated with impairments and other charges. And on top of this, it incurred a $0.87 hit involving inventory-related transaction charges.

On an adjusted basis, earnings per share still fell, dropping from $0.25 to $0.15. But at least that was in line with what analysts were hoping to see. Other profitability metrics were a mixed bag. Year over year, operating cash flow plummeted from $226 million down to $83 million. Normally, I would like to be able to calculate adjusted operating cash flow, where I strip out changes in working capital. But unfortunately, management has not provided enough detail to make that possible. On the other hand, EBITDA for the business improved markedly from $45 million to $61 million.

Even though the market was clearly unhappy with the earnings data provided by management, there was one really positive development that I am happy to share. When the company originally announced its plan to merge with MRC Global, it anticipated $70 million in annual run rate cost savings by the year 2028. That included $17 million this year and $42 million next year. However, the firm has bumped that up for 2026 from $17 million to $23 million. I wish I could say that this meant that the full-cost savings program would be even larger. But at this time, management does not expect this to be the case. While it certainly could occur, investors should instead interpret this as an acceleration of cost cutting. Also, for context, about half of all costs cut will come from eliminating duplicative corporate functions and IT systems. 27% will involve operational and supply chain initiatives. And the remaining 23% will come from the fact that we will have only one publicly traded company instead of two. The firm is also targeting cross-selling opportunities. But at this time, they have not committed to anything on that front.

In addition to the revenue and earnings per share falling short of expectations, I think the other thing that displeased the market was the fact that management decided to delay guidance for 2026. This stems from the fact that management is facing what it calls "persistent challenges" involving its ERP implementation within legacy MRC Global operations when it comes to the United States. Historically, ERP implementation can be incredibly costly. And technical hiccups are common, leading to cost overruns. The fact that management is not confident enough to provide guidance for 2026 is certainly discouraging. And when you add on top of this the fact that management expects overall revenue on a pro forma basis to be more or less flat this year, you can understand why market participants have turned pessimistic.

I wish I could say that having a comprehensive view of the overall financial picture of the combined company was easy. But the fact of the matter is that even if we take financial statements from MRC Global’s first nine months of its 2025 fiscal year and add that to DNOW’s results for 2025, we would still be missing about one month and five days of data for the acquired business. Some might chalk this up to a rounding error. But then when you add on top of this the absence of enough cash flow data to calculate out adjusted operating cash flow, I think the best way to evaluate the company is to base its potential off pro forma guidance that management made available to investors when the deal was initially announced.

In the image above, you can see what this guidance was. This includes, for context, the expected $70 million in annual run rate cost savings. If we strip that out from the equation, we would be looking at combined EBITDA of around $360 million and combined operating cash flow of about $445 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how the stock is priced at the moment. I included valuation data for synergies and without synergies. Even without them, the stock is incredibly cheap. And as that same chart illustrates, the company is trading drastically lower than other firms that have structural similarities to it. Admittedly, there are definite operational differences, such as areas of the economy that they emphasize. But even if you factor that in, the stock looks incredibly cheap in my opinion.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I would argue that DNOW is certainly an intriguing prospect. In fact, I would go so far as to say that, while I am frustrated by the lack of clarity provided by management, specifically when it comes to key financial metrics and guidance for 2026, shares of the business are trading cheap enough to justify some nice upside. So even though the stock is down materially, I would argue that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.
